levels/composite-arrow.rzk.md.body:
Every level so far was free: no hypothesis was needed. Genuine composition is different. A Segal type is one where each composable pair of arrows has a unique filler triangle, so `is-segal-A x y z f g` proves that the type of pairs `(h , triangle)` is contractible. Its center, `first (is-segal-A x y z f g)`, is the pair `(composite , witness)`. Take the first projection of that pair to get the composite arrow. Type the term and press Check.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
#def is-contr (A : U)
  : U
  := ОЈ (a : A) , (x : A) вҶ’ a =_{ A } x
#def is-segal (A : U)
  : U
  := (x : A) вҶ’ (y : A) вҶ’ (z : A) вҶ’ (f : hom A x y) вҶ’ (g : hom A y z)
   вҶ’ is-contr (ОЈ (h : hom A x z) , hom2 A x y z f g h)
```

```rzk template
#def compose
  (A : U) (is-segal-A : is-segal A) (x y z : A)
  (f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z)
  : hom A x z
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def compose
  (A : U) (is-segal-A : is-segal A) (x y z : A)
  (f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z)
  : hom A x z
  := first (first (is-segal-A x y z f g))
```

## Conclusion

The composite $g \circ f$ is the arrow at the center of the contractible space of fillers. The Segal condition is exactly what makes this arrow exist and be well-defined. Next, recover the triangle that witnesses it.

levels/composition-intro.md.body:
Every construction so far was free. **Composition** needs a hypothesis: a type is **Segal** when each composable pair of arrows has a unique filler triangle. The composite is read off the center of that contractible space.

*By the end you will be able to:* extract the composite arrow and recover the triangle that witnesses it.

levels/composition-summary.md.body: In a Segal type, composition exists and comes with a witnessing triangle, the two halves of one center of contraction. That is what makes a Segal type a **pre-$(\infty,1)$-category**. A **Rezk type** (a Segal type that is also locally univalent) is then a genuine $(\infty,1)$-category.

levels/composition-witness.rzk.md.body:
Building the composite arrow was only half of the center of contraction. Its second component is the triangle witnessing that the arrow really is the composite of $f$ and $g$. The goal's diagonal is the composite you built last time. Take the second projection of the center to recover its witness.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
#def is-contr (A : U)
  : U
  := ОЈ (a : A) , (x : A) вҶ’ a =_{ A } x
#def is-segal (A : U)
  : U
  := (x : A) вҶ’ (y : A) вҶ’ (z : A) вҶ’ (f : hom A x y) вҶ’ (g : hom A y z)
   вҶ’ is-contr (ОЈ (h : hom A x z) , hom2 A x y z f g h)
```

```rzk template
#def compose-witness
  (A : U) (is-segal-A : is-segal A) (x y z : A)
  (f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z)
  : hom2 A x y z f g (first (first (is-segal-A x y z f g)))
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def compose-witness
  (A : U) (is-segal-A : is-segal A) (x y z : A)
  (f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z)
  : hom2 A x y z f g (first (first (is-segal-A x y z f g)))
  := second (first (is-segal-A x y z f g))
```

## Conclusion

The composite and its witnessing triangle are the two halves of one center of contraction. The Segal hypothesis supplies both at once. The first projection is the arrow, the second is the triangle that proves it.

meta.statement: hom2 A x y z f g (first (first (is-segal-A x y z f g)))
meta.title: The composition witness

levels/constant-triangle.rzk.md.body:
A `hom2` is a triangle: a map out of the 2-simplex $\Delta^2$. The simplest one is constant: every edge is the identity at a single point $x$. Introduce the two coordinates, then find the point of $A$ that sits on all three edges.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
```

```rzk template
#def const-triangle (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom2 A x x x (id-hom A x) (id-hom A x) (id-hom A x)
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def const-triangle (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom2 A x x x (id-hom A x) (id-hom A x) (id-hom A x)
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ x
```

## Conclusion

Every boundary asked for $x$, so the constant function fills the whole triangle. In the next levels one edge becomes a genuine morphism, and the point has to vary along a coordinate.

inventory: id-hom : (A : U) вҶ’ (x : A) вҶ’ hom A x x | the identity arrow
meta.statement: hom2 A x x x (id-hom A x) (id-hom A x) (id-hom A x)
meta.title: The constant triangle

levels/forming-a-pair.rzk.md.body:
A dependent pair packages a point `a : A` with a point `b : B a` of the fiber above it. Build the pair.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
```

```rzk template
#def make-pair (A : U) (B : A вҶ’ U) (a : A) (b : B a)
  : ОЈ (x : A) , B x
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def make-pair (A : U) (B : A вҶ’ U) (a : A) (b : B a)
  : ОЈ (x : A) , B x
  := (a , b)
```

## Conclusion

The pair `(a , b)` inhabits the ОЈ-type. The first component chooses a point of the base, and the second lives in the fiber over it.

hints.text: A dependent pair is written `(a , b)`: the base point, then the fiber point.

levels/function-on-a-morphism.rzk.md.body:
Functions act on morphisms too. A morphism $f : x \to y$ in $A$ is a path. Applying $g$ at each moment of that path gives a morphism $g\,x \to g\,y$ in $B$. The function $g$ is already in place. Fill in the point of $A$ that $f$ traces out as the coordinate moves. Refine with `f`, then give the coordinate.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
```

```rzk template
#def ap-hom (A B : U) (g : A вҶ’ B) (x y : A) (f : hom A x y)
  : hom B (g x) (g y)
  := \ t вҶ’ g (?)
```

```rzk solution
#def ap-hom (A B : U) (g : A вҶ’ B) (x y : A) (f : hom A x y)
  : hom B (g x) (g y)
  := \ t вҶ’ g (f t)
```

## Conclusion

Applying $g$ along the path $f$ gives a morphism between the images. This is functoriality: a function carries morphisms to morphisms, here `g (f t)` tracing $g$'s image of $f$.

levels/function-on-a-point.rzk.md.body:
Now we leave a single type and bring in a function $g : A \to B$. A function sends each point of $A$ to a point of $B$. The identity morphism at a point just stays put, and $g$ carries it along. The application `g (?)` is already in place. Fill in the point of $A$ whose image is the identity's endpoint.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
```

```rzk template
#def map-point (A B : U) (g : A вҶ’ B) (x : A)
  : hom B (g x) (g x)
  := \ t вҶ’ g (?)
```

```rzk solution
#def map-point (A B : U) (g : A вҶ’ B) (x : A)
  : hom B (g x) (g x)
  := \ t вҶ’ g (x)
```

## Conclusion

A function sends a point to a point, and the constant path at `g x` is its identity. The next level carries a whole morphism along, not just a point.

levels/functions-act-on-paths.rzk.md.body:
A function `f : A вҶ’ B` respects identifications: a path `p : x = y` gives a path `f x = f y`. Build it by path induction, reducing to the case where `p` is `refl` and both ends are `f x`.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def ind-path
  ( A : U)
  ( a : A)
  ( C : (x : A) вҶ’ (a = x) вҶ’ U)
  ( d : C a refl)
  ( x : A)
  ( p : a = x)
  : C x p
  := idJ (A , a , C , d , x , p)
```

```rzk template
#def ap (A B : U) (x y : A) (f : A вҶ’ B) (p : x = y)
  : f x = f y
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def ap (A B : U) (x y : A) (f : A вҶ’ B) (p : x = y)
  : f x = f y
  := ind-path A x (\ y' p' вҶ’ f x = f y') refl y p
```

## Conclusion

This is `ap`, the action of a function on a path. When `p` is `refl`, so is its image, which is exactly the base case path induction asks for.

hints.text: Use `ind-path` to reduce to the case where the path is `refl`. You supply a motive: for an endpoint and a path to it, what are you proving?
The motive sends an endpoint `b` and a path `x = b` to the goal `f x = f b`. At `refl` that is `f x = f x`, so the base case is `refl`. Build it with `ind-path A x (\ b q вҶ’ f x = f b) refl y p`.

inventory: ind-path : (A : U) (a : A) (C : (b : A) вҶ’ a = b вҶ’ U) (d : C a refl) (x : A) (p : a = x) вҶ’ C x p | path induction
meta.title: Functions act on paths

levels/functions-intro.md.body:
A function $g : A \to B$ does more than map points: it carries whole cells. Applying $g$ along a path gives a path, so a function acts on morphisms, not just points. This is **functoriality**.

This section opens with a quick **pre-test**. If functoriality is new to you, say so: you will get a pointer to review first, and the dependent level will wait for you.

*By the end you will be able to:* carry a point and a morphism along a function.

levels/functions-summary.md.body: A function preserves cells: it sends points to points and morphisms to morphisms. With functoriality in hand, the last section tackles genuine composition.

levels/identity-morphism.rzk.md.body:
A morphism $x \to y$ in $A$ is a path along the directed interval $\Delta^1$. The simplest one is the identity: the morphism from $x$ to itself that just stays put. Both endpoints of the path are $x$, so a constant path will do. Build it.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
```

```rzk template
#def identity (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def identity (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
```

## Conclusion

The constant path is the identity morphism. Both endpoints ask for $x$, so $x$ itself fills the hole. There is no need to move along the interval at all.

hints.text: Both endpoints of the path are the same point $x$, so you never need to move along the interval.
Right now the goal is still the whole path type `(t : 2 | О”В№ t) вҶ’ вҖҰ`. Your first move is to introduce the interval coordinate: tap `О»-intro` or type `\ t вҶ’ ?`. Then return $x$.

inventory: id-hom : (A : U) вҶ’ (x : A) вҶ’ hom A x x | the identity arrow (an alternative)
meta.title: The identity morphism

levels/left-unit-triangle.rzk.md.body:
Now the mirror image. Given $f : x \to y$, the triangle whose left edge is the identity at $x$ also has $f$ as its hypotenuse, but this time the degenerate copy of $f$ must vary in the other coordinate. Build it.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
```

```rzk template
#def left-unit (A : U) (x y : A) (f : hom A x y)
  : hom2 A x x y (id-hom A x) f f
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ ?
```

```rzk solution
#def left-unit (A : U) (x y : A) (f : hom A x y)
  : hom2 A x x y (id-hom A x) f f
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ f s
```

## Conclusion

The same edge $f$, reparametrized in the other coordinate. The right-unit triangle used the first coordinate. The left-unit one uses the second.

inventory: id-hom : (A : U) вҶ’ (x : A) вҶ’ hom A x x | the identity arrow
meta.statement: hom2 A x x y (id-hom A x) f f
meta.title: The left-unit triangle

levels/morphisms-intro.md.body:
In directed type theory a **morphism** $x \to y$ is a path along the directed interval $\Delta^1$, and a **triangle** (`hom2`) is a map out of the $2$-simplex $\Delta^2$. This first section builds them by hand.

*By the end you will be able to:* construct the identity morphism, fill the constant triangle, and reparametrize an edge to fill a degenerate (unit) triangle. The mirror **left-unit** triangle is marked вҳ… (optional enrichment you may skip).

levels/morphisms-summary.md.body: You can now build morphisms as paths and fill triangles by reusing an edge under a change of coordinates. These degenerate triangles needed no extra hypotheses. The next section brings in functions.

levels/path-concatenation.rzk.md.body:
Two paths `p : x = y` and `q : y = z` compose into a path `x = z`. Induct on the second path `q`. When `q` is `refl`, the endpoint `z` is `y`, and you already hold `p : x = y`.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def ind-path
  ( A : U)
  ( a : A)
  ( C : (x : A) вҶ’ (a = x) вҶ’ U)
  ( d : C a refl)
  ( x : A)
  ( p : a = x)
  : C x p
  := idJ (A , a , C , d , x , p)
```

```rzk template
#def concat (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : x = y) (q : y = z)
  : x = z
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def concat (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : x = y) (q : y = z)
  : x = z
  := ind-path A y (\ z' q' вҶ’ x = z') p z q
```

## Conclusion

This is `concat`. The base case is `p` itself, not `refl`: fixing `q` at `refl` leaves the first path untouched.

hints.text: Induct on the second path `q` with `ind-path`, keeping `p` fixed. When `q` is `refl`, the endpoint is `y` and you already hold `p`.
The motive sends an endpoint `c` and a path `y = c` to `x = c`. The base case is `p` itself, not `refl`. Build it with `ind-path A y (\ c r вҶ’ x = c) p z q`.

inventory: ind-path : (A : U) (a : A) (C : (b : A) вҶ’ a = b вҶ’ U) (d : C a refl) (x : A) (p : a = x) вҶ’ C x p | path induction
meta.title: Path concatenation

levels/path-on-first-components.rzk.md.body:
Two pairs are identified by a path `e : s = t`. Their first components are then identified too. Apply `ap` with the first projection to turn the path of pairs into a path `first s = first t`.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def ind-path
  ( A : U)
  ( a : A)
  ( C : (x : A) вҶ’ (a = x) вҶ’ U)
  ( d : C a refl)
  ( x : A)
  ( p : a = x)
  : C x p
  := idJ (A , a , C , d , x , p)
#def ap
  ( A B : U) ( x y : A) ( f : A вҶ’ B) ( p : x = y)
  : f x = f y
  := ind-path A x (\ y' p' вҶ’ f x = f y') refl y p
```

```rzk template
#def first-path-ОЈ (A : U) (B : A вҶ’ U) (s t : ОЈ (a : A) , B a) (e : s = t)
  : first s = first t
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def first-path-ОЈ (A : U) (B : A вҶ’ U) (s t : ОЈ (a : A) , B a) (e : s = t)
  : first s = first t
  := ap (ОЈ (a : A) , B a) A s t (\ z вҶ’ first z) e
```

## Conclusion

This is `first-path-ОЈ`, the action of `first` on a path of pairs. It is `ap` applied to the projection `\ z вҶ’ first z`. The matching statement for second components needs transport, which we leave to the library.

hints.text: A path `e : s = t` between pairs, pushed through the projection `first`, gives a path between first components. That pushing-through is `ap`.
Apply `ap` with the function `\ z вҶ’ first z`: `ap (ОЈ (a : A) , B a) A s t (\ z вҶ’ first z) e`.

inventory: ap : (A B : U) (x y : A) (f : A вҶ’ B) (p : x = y) вҶ’ f x = f y | apply a function to a path
meta.title: A path on first components

levels/path-reversal.rzk.md.body:
A path runs from `x` to `y`. Reverse it to get a path from `y` to `x`. By path induction it is enough to reverse `refl`, and the reverse of the trivial path is trivial.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def ind-path
  ( A : U)
  ( a : A)
  ( C : (x : A) вҶ’ (a = x) вҶ’ U)
  ( d : C a refl)
  ( x : A)
  ( p : a = x)
  : C x p
  := idJ (A , a , C , d , x , p)
```

```rzk template
#def rev (A : U) (x y : A) (p : x = y)
  : y = x
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def rev (A : U) (x y : A) (p : x = y)
  : y = x
  := ind-path A x (\ y' p' вҶ’ y' = x) refl y p
```

## Conclusion

Reversal turns `x = y` into `y = x`. Every path is invertible, which is why identifications behave like isomorphisms.

hints.text: Induct on `p` with `ind-path`. As the endpoint varies, you want a path back to `x`.
The motive sends an endpoint `b` and a path `x = b` to `b = x`. At `refl` that is `x = x`, so the base case is `refl`. Build it with `ind-path A x (\ b q вҶ’ b = x) refl y p`.

inventory: ind-path : (A : U) (a : A) (C : (b : A) вҶ’ a = b вҶ’ U) (d : C a refl) (x : A) (p : a = x) вҶ’ C x p | path induction
meta.title: Path reversal

levels/paths-intro.md.body:
This chapter builds the homotopy-type-theory toolkit the later proofs lean on, starting with **paths**. A path `p : x = y` identifies two points of a type, and `refl` is the trivial path from a point to itself. The key move is **path induction**: to prove something about an arbitrary path, it is enough to prove it for `refl`.

These lemmas are standard, so most are marked as pre-tests. If you already know your path algebra, mark them known and move on. Path concatenation and the zig-zag stay mandatory, and the zig-zag is the move the later naturality proofs need.

*By the end you will be able to:* reverse a path, concatenate paths, apply a function to a path, and join two paths that share an endpoint.

levels/paths-summary.md.body: You now have the basic path algebra: reversal, concatenation, the action of a function on a path, and the two ways to join paths that share an endpoint. These are the moves the directed proofs use later, where the unit laws and the Yoneda round-trips are equalities built by path induction.

levels/reflexivity.rzk.md.body:
A **path** `p : x = y` identifies two points of a type. The simplest one is `refl`, the trivial path from a point to itself. Build the path from `x` to `x`.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
```

```rzk template
#def refl-path (A : U) (x : A)
  : x = x
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def refl-path (A : U) (x : A)
  : x = x
  := refl
```

## Conclusion

`refl` is the trivial identification. Path induction lets every proof about a general path reduce to this one case.

hints.text: Every point has a trivial path to itself. That path is `refl`.

levels/right-unit-triangle.rzk.md.body:
Now an edge becomes a genuine morphism. The hypotenuse of a `hom2` is the composite of its other two edges. Most triangles need $A$ to be Segal, but some are free. Given $f : x \to y$, the triangle whose right edge is the identity at $y$ has $f$ itself as its hypotenuse. This time the point must vary along the first coordinate. Build it.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def О”В№
  : 2 вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ t вҶ’ TOP
#def О”ВІ
  : ( 2 Г— 2) вҶ’ TOPE
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ s вүӨ t
#def hom (A : U) (x y : A)
  : U
  := (t : О”В№) вҶ’ A [ t вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ x , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ y ]
#def id-hom (A : U) (x : A)
  : hom A x x
  := \ t вҶ’ x
#def hom2 (A : U) (x y z : A)
  ( f : hom A x y) (g : hom A y z) (h : hom A x z)
  : U
  := ((t , s) : О”ВІ) вҶ’ A [ s вүЎ 0вӮӮ вҶҰ f t , t вүЎ 1вӮӮ вҶҰ g s , s вүЎ t вҶҰ h s ]
```

```rzk template
#def right-unit (A : U) (x y : A) (f : hom A x y)
  : hom2 A x y y f (id-hom A y) f
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ ?
```

```rzk solution
#def right-unit (A : U) (x y : A) (f : hom A x y)
  : hom2 A x y y f (id-hom A y) f
  := \ (t , s) вҶ’ f t
```

## Conclusion

The degenerate triangle is just $f$ ignoring the second coordinate. Reusing an existing edge, reparametrized, is the bread and butter of simplicial proofs.

hints.text: The right edge is the identity at $y$, so the whole triangle is just $f$, reparametrized. You only need one coordinate.
Look at the bottom edge of the goal: `вҶҰ f t`. That tells you to apply $f$ to the first coordinate. Type `\ (t , s) вҶ’ f t`.

inventory: id-hom : (A : U) вҶ’ (x : A) вҶ’ hom A x x | the identity arrow
meta.statement: hom2 A x y y f (id-hom A y) f
meta.title: The right-unit triangle

levels/second-projection.rzk.md.body:
A pair has two projections. The first, `first p`, is its point of the base `A`. The second, `second p`, lives in the fiber `B (first p)`. Recover the second component.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
```

```rzk template
#def snd (A : U) (B : A вҶ’ U) (p : ОЈ (x : A) , B x)
  : B (first p)
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def snd (A : U) (B : A вҶ’ U) (p : ОЈ (x : A) , B x)
  : B (first p)
  := second p
```

## Conclusion

`second p` lives in `B (first p)`, the fiber over the first projection. The type of the second component depends on the first.

hints.text: The second projection of a pair `p` is `second p`. Its type is the fiber `B (first p)`.

levels/sigma-intro.md.body:
A **ОЈ-type** `ОЈ (x : A), B x` is the type of dependent pairs: a point `x : A` together with a point of `B x`. Its projections `first` and `second` recover the two halves. This section forms pairs, projects them, and shows that a path between pairs gives a path between their first components.

These are standard, so the openers are pre-tests. The path-on-components puzzle stays mandatory. It is the move the later uniqueness-of-composites argument rests on.

*By the end you will be able to:* form a dependent pair, project out its components, and turn a path of pairs into a path of first components.

levels/sigma-summary.md.body: You can now form dependent pairs, project them, and push a path of pairs down to a path of first components. The last is `first-path-ОЈ`, built from `ap`. The uniqueness of composites in a Segal type, later, rests on exactly this move.

levels/zag-zig-concatenation.rzk.md.body:
The mirror image: `p : y = x` and `q : y = z` share their domain `y`. Reverse `p` and concatenate with `q` to get a path `x = z`.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def ind-path
  ( A : U)
  ( a : A)
  ( C : (x : A) вҶ’ (a = x) вҶ’ U)
  ( d : C a refl)
  ( x : A)
  ( p : a = x)
  : C x p
  := idJ (A , a , C , d , x , p)
#def rev
  ( A : U) ( x y : A) ( p : x = y)
  : y = x
  := ind-path A x (\ y' p' вҶ’ y' = x) refl y p
#def concat
  ( A : U) ( x y z : A) ( p : x = y) ( q : y = z)
  : x = z
  := ind-path A y (\ z' q' вҶ’ x = z') p z q
```

```rzk template
#def zag-zig-concat (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : y = x) (q : y = z)
  : x = z
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def zag-zig-concat (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : y = x) (q : y = z)
  : x = z
  := concat A x y z (rev A y x p) q
```

## Conclusion

The two zig-zag concatenations handle paths meeting at a shared endpoint, whichever end it is.

hints.text: You have `p : y = x` and `q : y = z`, both starting at `y`. Reverse `p`, then concatenate with `q`.
`rev A y x p : x = y`, so `concat A x y z (rev A y x p) q : x = z`.

inventory: rev    : (A : U) (x y : A) (p : x = y) вҶ’ y = x | reverse a path
concat : (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : x = y) (q : y = z) вҶ’ x = z | concatenate two paths
meta.title: Zag-zig concatenation

levels/zig-zag-concatenation.rzk.md.body:
Two paths can point at the same target: `p : x = y` and `q : z = y` both end at `y`. To join them into a path `x = z`, reverse `q` and concatenate. This is the move the later naturality proof leans on.

```rzk prelude
#lang rzk-1
#def ind-path
  ( A : U)
  ( a : A)
  ( C : (x : A) вҶ’ (a = x) вҶ’ U)
  ( d : C a refl)
  ( x : A)
  ( p : a = x)
  : C x p
  := idJ (A , a , C , d , x , p)
#def rev
  ( A : U) ( x y : A) ( p : x = y)
  : y = x
  := ind-path A x (\ y' p' вҶ’ y' = x) refl y p
#def concat
  ( A : U) ( x y z : A) ( p : x = y) ( q : y = z)
  : x = z
  := ind-path A y (\ z' q' вҶ’ x = z') p z q
```

```rzk template
#def zig-zag-concat (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : x = y) (q : z = y)
  : x = z
  := ?
```

```rzk solution
#def zig-zag-concat (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : x = y) (q : z = y)
  : x = z
  := concat A x y z p (rev A z y q)
```

## Conclusion

Reversing one path lets you concatenate two that share a codomain. The mirror case, paths that share a domain, is the optional next level.

hints.text: You have `p : x = y` and `q : z = y`, both ending at `y`. Reverse `q`, then concatenate.
`rev A z y q : y = z`, so `concat A x y z p (rev A z y q) : x = z`.

inventory: rev    : (A : U) (x y : A) (p : x = y) вҶ’ y = x | reverse a path
concat : (A : U) (x y z : A) (p : x = y) (q : y = z) вҶ’ x = z | concatenate two paths
meta.title: Zig-zag concatenation
